author: Shillim Institute in collaboration with ATREE
artistdescription: Located in the Western Ghats, The Shillim Institute safeguards approximately 2000 acres of land in the Northern region of this mountain range, which has been declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a biodiversity conservation hotspot. The Institute has enlisted local communities as forest guards, and introduced thousands of native plant species, resulting in the flourishing of over a million trees comprising 64 diverse species. The Pavna Collective is a consortium of conservation, ecology, and arts organizations, convened by the Shillim Institute. Among its initiatives, the Pavna Collective sponsors 3-6 art residencies a year, fellowships,Mapping Workshop, Cultural documentation and Skill development programs in the Sayadri ranges.

projects.title: Green Squad : Know Your Local Biodiversity

sections.value:
The session began with an introduction to biodiversity through a short presentation focusing on local species and ecosystems. In some sessions, documentary screenings were also used to help students better understand biodiversity and conservation. This was followed by safety guidelines and responsible behaviour during nature exploration. Students then participated in nature trails across different landscapes, including school campuses, community areas, and nearby wild habitats. During these walks, they observed local plants and animals, noted local names, and discussed the ecological importance of species.

Students also collected fallen leaves and prepared herbariums using local plant species, helping them learn plant identification and documentation. Midway through the trail, students explored seasonal wild fruits such as Karvand, Toran, and Jambhul, encouraging familiarity with local biodiversity.

The session concluded with group discussions and reflection, where students shared observations and documented their findings through drawings and notes, strengthening their understanding and appreciation of local biodiversity.  Through biodiversity sessions and nature trails, students gained a closer understanding of the natural environment around them. Exploring the school campus, nearby community areas, and wild habitats helped students notice small but important elements of biodiversity such as plants, flowers, insects, birds, and animals. Many students observed edible fruits, seasonal plants, and local species that they had seen before but never paid attention to. This hands-on experience helped them connect with their surroundings and develop curiosity about local biodiversity.

Students documented their observations through drawings, lists of local flora and fauna, and herbarium preparation using fallen leaves. These activities encouraged careful observation and helped students identify differences between plant species. Preparing herbariums also introduced them to basic documentation and research skills. Students began asking questions, comparing plants, and discussing the role of biodiversity in maintaining ecosystems.

The sessions improved students’ understanding of how plants and animals are interconnected and why biodiversity is important. They also developed confidence in observing and recording nature. Over time, students showed increased awareness and responsibility toward protecting local biodiversity. Many shared their learning with friends and family, helping spread awareness within their communities.

Overall, the activity helped students gain insights into local biodiversity, improve observation and documentation skills, and build appreciation for nature, encouraging them to become more responsible towards their local environment.
